risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to either the hydro-cortisone (n = 31: 21 males, 10 females) or placebo (n = 33: 21 males, 12 females) groups." and "Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized/randomly assigned, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated list, random number table, block randomization). With no direct method or contextual clue about sequence generation, this domain is unclear rather than high risk.

Quote: "Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo." Comment: The report states that participants were randomly assigned and that the trial was double-blind, but it does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes) preventing the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo." and "Following consent, the nurse administered the first oral dose [20 mg hydrocortisone (Cortef, Pharmacia) or placebo capsules] within 12 h of hospital admission." Comment: The study explicitly states it was conducted in double-blind fashion and used placebo capsules, indicating blinding of participants and treating personnel was intended and likely maintained. Because the main outcomes (PTSD symptoms, depression, quality of life) are subjective and could be influenced by knowledge of allocation, blinding is important here; the reported double-blind placebo-controlled design supports a Low risk judgement.

Quote: "Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo." Quote: "Participants were administered the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS) to assess incidence of PTSD and PTSD symptoms... Participants were left a packet of self-report measures... Self-report measures included the Center for Epidemiological Studies—Depression Scale... and the SF-36 to assess health-related quality of life." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by clinician interviewers (CAPS) and by patient self-report (CES-D, SF-36). Because the trial is explicitly described as double-blind, outcome assessors and participants were likely blinded to allocation; the key subjective outcomes therefore were not obviously vulnerable to detection bias from knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "Fifty-one participants (78%) were retained at the 1-month follow-up, and 42 (65%) were retained at the 3-month follow-up. There was no differential drop out between the hydrocortisone and placebo groups. At 1 month posttrauma, 6 participants had dropped from the placebo group and 7 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group, while at 3 months a total of 9 had dropped from the placebo group and 12 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group." and "A series of repeated measures, or analyses of covariance (ANCOVAs), were conducted on participants who completed the entire study protocol (completer analyses)..." Comment: For the main PTSD follow-up outcome, attrition by 3 months was 21/64 overall (32.8%), exceeding the usual high-risk threshold, with imbalance between arms (hydrocortisone 12/31 = 38.7% vs placebo 9/33 = 27.3%; difference about 11.4 percentage points). The outcome analysis was based on completers rather than an ITT/missing-data approach. Although most reasons were inability to contact participants, the reported magnitude and imbalance of missing outcome data at the analysed time point are sufficient positive evidence for attrition bias.
